perturbation.original_text: At a minimum, such policies must include the following: (a) commercial general liability insurance, completed-operations and independent-contractors coverage in the amount of $1,000,000, per person/per occurrence for bodily injury and property damage combined with a general aggregate of $3,000,000.
perturbation.changed_text: At a minimum, such policies may include the following: (a) commercial general liability insurance.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause mandates specific insurance coverage, providing financial protection for both parties. Removing the specific coverage amount introduces legal risk by allowing for inadequate insurance, potentially violating Florida's minimum insurance requirements for businesses.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statute § 627.4147 - Minimum Commercial Liability Insurance Coverage
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute § 627.4147 mandates specific minimum commercial liability coverage for businesses. The modified text removes the mandated coverage amount, creating a contract that permits lower coverage than legally required in Florida.

Home > Laws > 2011 Florida Statutes > Title XXXVII > Chapter 627 > Section 4147 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2011 Florida Statutes Title XXXVII INSURANCE Chapter 627 INSURANCE RATES AND CONTRACTS Entire Chapter SECTION 4147 Medical malpractice insurance contracts. F.S. 627.4147 627.4147 Medical malpractice insurance contracts. — (1) In addition to any other requirements imposed by law, each self-insurance policy as authorized under s. 627.357 or s. 624.462 or insurance policy providing coverage for claims arising out of the rendering of, or the failure to render, medical care or services, including those of the Florida Medical Malpractice Joint Underwriting Association, shall include: (a) A clause requiring the insured to cooperate fully in the review process prescribed under s. 766.106 if a notice of intent to file a claim for medical malpractice is made against the insured. (b)1. A clause clearly stating whether or not the insured has the exclusive right to veto any offer of admission of liability and for arbitration pursuant to s. 766.106 , settlement offer, or offer of judgment if the offer is within policy limits. An insurer or self-insurer shall not make or conclude, without the permission of the insured, any offer of admission of liability and for arbitration pursuant to s. 766.106 , settlement offer, or offer of judgment, if such offer is outside the policy limits. However, any offer for admission of liability and for arbitration made under s. 766.106 , settlement offer, or offer of judgment made by an insurer or self-insurer shall be made in good faith and in the best interest of the insured. 2. If the policy contains a clause stating the insured does not have the exclusive right to veto any offer or admission of liability and for arbitration made pursuant to s. 766.106 , settlement offer or offer of judgment, the insurer or self-insurer shall provide to the insured or the insured’s legal representative by certified mail, return receipt requested, a copy of the final offer of admission of liability and for arbitration made pursuant to s. 766.106 , settlement offer or offer of judgment and at the same time such offer is provided to the claimant. A copy of any final agreement reached between the insurer and claimant shall also be p
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates specific insurance coverage amounts, while the changed text makes such coverage optional, removing the specific financial protection initially in place. This creates a potential legal contradiction because the modified contract might not meet Florida's minimum insurance requirements for businesses. The Law Explanation states that Florida Statute § 627.4147 mandates specific minimum commercial liability coverage, and the scraped law snippet 1, referring to F.S. 627.4147, discusses medical malpractice insurance contracts, indicating that specific insurance requirements are legally mandated.

perturbation.original_text: By the dates specified below, an approved insurance company must issue a certificate of insurance showing compliance with the insurance requirements in this Section 6.19 [Insurance] and you must furnish us with a paid receipt showing the certificate number: (a) 30 days before beginning construction of the Premises; (b) if the Premises are constructed and presently owned or leased by you, 10 days from the Agreement Date; or (c) if the Premises are not presently owned or leased, 10 days after ownership of the Premises is conveyed to you or you sign a lease for the Premises. The certificate of insurance must include a statement by the insurer that the policy or policies may not be canceled, subject to nonrenewal, or materially altered without at least 30 days' prior written notice to us. Upon our request, you must supply us with copies of all insurance policies and proof of payment. Every year, you must send us current certificates of insurance and copies of all insurance policies.
perturbation.changed_text: By the dates specified below, an insurance company must issue a certificate of insurance showing compliance with the insurance requirements in this Section 6.19 [Insurance]. Upon our request, you must supply us with copies of all insurance policies. Every year, you may send us current certificates of insurance and copies of all insurance policies.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates that the insurance company be 'approved' and that the certificate include a statement that the policy cannot be cancelled or altered without 30 days notice. By removing this language, it allows the franchisee to obtain insurance policies that are easily cancelled or altered without notice, which can cause the franchisor damages due to being uninsured without their knowledge. The law requires adequate notice to protect interested parties.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statute § 626.431 - Notice of Cancellation, Nonrenewal, or Change of Coverage
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute § 626.431 outlines requirements for insurers regarding notice of cancellation, nonrenewal, or change of coverage. The modified contract language removes the requirement for the insurer to provide a statement ensuring notice of cancellation, potentially conflicting with the statutory requirement to notify interested parties.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes critical consumer protection mandates found in the original text. Specifically, it omits the requirement for an 'approved' insurance company and the mandate for a statement ensuring 30 days' notice before cancellation or alteration of the policy. According to the perturbation explanation, this omission allows the franchisee to obtain insurance policies that are easily cancelled or altered without notice, potentially harming the franchisor. The law explanation further clarifies that Florida Statute § 626.431 requires insurers to provide notice of cancellation, nonrenewal, or change of coverage. Since the altered text removes the stipulation for the insurance company to guarantee this notice, a clear contradiction arises as the contract now lacks a legally mandated consumer protection, aligning with the definition of an Omission Legal Contradiction.
